content.problemStatement:
Alice and Bob have NNN cards with them. The iii-th card has the number AiA_iAi​ written on its front, and BiB_iBi​ written on its back.

It's guaranteed that Ai≠AjA_i \neq A_jAi​=Aj​ for i≠ji \neq ji=j, i.e, all the AiA_iAi​ values are distinct.

All the cards are face-up, so only their AiA_iAi​ values are visible.

Alice will choose exactly one card from the NNN cards, and remove it.

After Alice has chosen her card, Bob will choose a card from the remaining N−1N-1N−1 cards.

Bob is greedy, and wants his card to have a large number on it. However, since he can only see the AiA_iAi​ values, he will always choose whichever card has the **maximum** AiA_iAi​ value among the remaining cards.

If Alice chooses card xxx, and Bob chooses card yyy, Alice will win if and only if

max⁡(Ax,Bx)>max⁡(Ay,By)\max(A_x, B_x) \gt \max(A_y, B_y)max(Ax​,Bx​)>max(Ay​,By​)Is there a way for Alice to choose a card such that she wins?


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of three lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN — the number of cards.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ — the numbers written on the fronts of the cards.
The third line contains NNN space-separated integers B1,B2,…,BNB_1, B_2, \ldots, B_NB1​,B2​,…,BN​ — the numbers written on the backs of the cards.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the answer: `"Yes"` if Alice can win, and `"No"` otherwise (without quotes).


Each character of the output may be printed in either uppercase or lowercase, i.e, the strings `No`, `NO`, `no`, and `nO` will all be treated as equivalent.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 2⋅1052\cdot 10^52⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: Alice can choose the second card, forcing Bob to choose the first.
max⁡(A2,B2)=4>3=max⁡(A1,B1)\max(A_2, B_2) = 4 \gt 3 = \max(A_1, B_1)max(A2​,B2​)=4>3=max(A1​,B1​), so Alice wins.
Test case 222: No matter which card Alice chooses, max⁡(Ai,Bi)=3\max(A_i, B_i) = 3max(Ai​,Bi​)=3.
Alice needs the value to be strictly greater to win; since they're equal here, she can't win.
Test case 444: Alice can choose the first card, and Bob will choose the last (since 555 is the largest remaining AiA_iAi​ value).
max⁡(A1,B1)=6>5=max⁡(A4,B4)\max(A_1, B_1) = 6 \gt 5 = \max(A_4, B_4)max(A1​,B1​)=6>5=max(A4​,B4​), so Alice wins.
Note that Alice wouldn't have won if Bob chose the third card, since max⁡(A3,B3)=7>6\max(A_3, B_3) = 7 \gt 6max(A3​,B3​)=7>6.
However, A3=1A_3 = 1A3​=1 isn't the largest remaining AiA_iAi​ value after Alice's choice, so Bob won't choose this card.
